elements.sent-2411-001: This paper discusses topological twisting of $d=4$ $\CN=2$ field theories.
elements.sent-2411-002: The first example of such a theory was discovered by Witten \cite{Witten:1988ze , an example which had momentous implications.}
elements.sent-2411-003: The study of topologically twisted $d=4$ $\CN=2$ field theories is interesting in physics because it provides examples of computable partition functions and correlation functions within nontrivial $d=4$ QFTs.
elements.sent-2411-004: These ``topological correlators'' have been interesting to mathematicians because they provide invariants of smooth four-manifolds.\footnote{In this paper we work with ``standard'' four-manifolds: They are smooth, compact manifolds without boundary.}
elements.sent-2411-005: They will be orientable and oriented, although a choice of orientation is an example of a ``field,'' as discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-006: We will denote a typical standard four-manifold by $\IX$.
elements.sent-2411-007: More precisely, they provide invariants of smooth four-manifolds equipped with extra topological data.
elements.sent-2411-008: One goal of this paper is to clarify exactly what that extra topological data is.
elements.sent-2411-009: In Donaldson-Witten theory (based on $\mr{SU(2)}$ $\CN=2$ supersymmetric Yang-Mills theory) the topological correlators only depend on the orientation and the diffeomorphism type of $\IX$ and a choice of 't Hooft flux for the gauge group.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, the example of $\CN=2^*$ theories shows that in general, the correlators depend on further topological data.
elements.sent-2411-011: In this example, topological twisting requires the introduction of an ``ultraviolet'' spin$^\mr{c}$ structure \cite{Labastida:1997rg,Manschot:2021qqe .}
elements.sent-2411-013: How does one topologically twist an arbitrary $d=4$ $\CN=2$ field theory, and what topological data do the correlators depend on?.
elements.sent-2411-014: This paper answers that question for all $d=4$ $\CN=2$ theories and illustrates the general construction for renormalizable Lagrangian theories and for some theories of class $\CS$.
elements.sent-2411-015: There are many viewpoints on what should be meant by the general notion of ``topological twisting.'' They all generalize the original example in \cite{Witten:1988ze .}
elements.sent-2411-016: It would be interesting to relate the twistings we describe to the general viewpoint advocated in \cite{Elliott:2018cbx,Elliott:2020ecf,Elliott:2024jvw .}
elements.sent-2411-017: We will view topological twisting as a specification of background fields such that a supersymmetry, denoted $\CQ$, remains unbroken.
elements.sent-2411-018: Since we do not work in a Hamiltonian framework, we view the supersymmetry as an odd vectorfield on the supermanifold of field configurations.
elements.sent-2411-019: We assume $\CQ$ is a scalar since general manifolds $\IX$ do not possess covariantly constant vectors, spinors, etc. $\CQ$ must square to zero on physical operators (and, in the Hamiltonian formulation, on states).
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, in a gauge theory, ``physical operators'' are the gauge invariant operators.
elements.sent-2411-021: In general, there can be different ``inequivalent'' topological twistings of a given theory.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, there are, famously, several inequivalent twistings of $\CN=4$ super Yang-Mills (SYM) \cite{Vafa:1994tf (which is a special case of an $\CN=2$ SYM), as we recall in section \ref{sec:3twist_n=4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In our view, these are 3 inequivalent choices of background fields effecting a topological twisting.
elements.sent-2411-024: The specification of background fields effecting a topological twisting will be given using the mathematical concept of transfer of structure group.\footnote{In an unpublished note, \cite{Freed:TopTwistUnpublished , D.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Freed interprets Witten's topological twisting in the framework of the ideas of Cartan and Klein, a viewpoint close in spirit to that taken in this paper.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Three Inequivalent Twistings of 4d $\CN=4$ Super Yang-Mills Theory
elements.sent-2411-367: In this section, we describe the three inequivalent twistings of 4d, $\CN=4$ super Yang-Mills theory discussed in \cite{Yamron:1988qc,Vafa:1994tf,Marcus:1995mq,Dabholkar:2020fde , using the transfer of structure group.}
elements.sent-2411-368: The three twisting homomorphisms are based on the three homomorphisms $\mr{Spin(4) \rightarrow \mr{S U(4)}$ discussed in \cite{Vafa:1994tf :.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: The first homomorphism $\mr{Spin(4) \rightarrow \mr{S U(4)}$ is chosen such that the 4 of $\mr{SU(4)}$ decomposes to $(2,1) \oplus(1,2)$ under pullback.}
elements.sent-2411-370: This corresponds to the Geometric Langlands (GL) twist \cite{Kapustin:2006pk , originally discussed in \cite{Yamron:1988qc,Marcus:1995mq .}}
elements.sent-2411-371: The second homomorphism $\mr{Spin(4) \rightarrow \mr{S U(4)}$ is chosen such that the 4 of $\mr{SU(4)}$ decomposes to $(1,2) \oplus(1,1) \oplus(1,1)$ under pullback.}
elements.sent-2411-372: This corresponds to the usual Donaldson-Witten (DW) twist, thinking of the $\CN=4$ theory as an $\CN=2$ theory \cite{Witten:1988ze .}
elements.sent-2411-373: The third homomorphism $\mr{Spin(4) \rightarrow \mr{S U(4)}$ is chosen such that the 4 of $\mr{SU(4)}$ decomposes to $(1,2) \oplus(1,2)$ under pullback.}
elements.sent-2411-374: This corresponds to the Vafa-Witten (VW) twist \cite{Vafa:1994tf .}
elements.sent-2411-375: We now describe the twisting group $G^{\mr{t w }$ and twisting homomorphisms $\varphi^{\mr{t w : G^{\mr{t w \to G^{\mr {phys }$ in each case.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-376: The notation in this section follows Example \ref{ex:4dN=4 above.}
elements.sent-2411-377: Define \begin{equation}\label{eq:G-and-C-GL-Twist} \begin{aligned} \wt{G}_1^{\mr{tw}}=\mr{Spin(4)} \times \mr{U(1)_R}\times \wt{G}^{\mr{gauge}} C_1^{\mr{t w}}=\{(\zeta, \zeta, 1, g):(\zeta, \zeta, \zeta, g) \in C^{\mr{p h y s}}\}~. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The twisting group is $G_1^{\mr{t w =\wt{G _1^{\mr{t w / C_1^{\mr{t w }$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-045: \begin{equation}\label{eq:G-and-C-GL-Twist}  \wt{G}_1^{\mr{tw}}=\mr{Spin(4)} \times \mr{U(1)_R}\times \wt{G}^{\mr{gauge}} C_1^{\mr{t w}}=\{(\zeta, \zeta, 1, g):(\zeta, \zeta, \zeta, g) \in C^{\mr{p h y s}}\}~. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-378: The twisting homomorphism is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-GL-Twist} \begin{aligned} \varphi_1^{\mr{GL}}: \wt{G}_1^{\mr {tw}} \longrightarrow \wt{G}^{\mr {phys }} \big(\left(u_1, u_2\right), e^{i \theta},g\big) \longmapsto\big(\left(u_1, u_2\right), \mr{diag}\big(e^{i \theta} u_1, e^{-i \theta} u_2\big),g\big)~, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\left(u_1, u_2\right) \in \mr{SU(2) \times \mr{SU(2) \cong \mr{Spin(4) .$ Then $\varphi_1^{\mr{GL }$ descends to a homomorphism \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-GL-Twist-bar} \ov{\varphi}_1^{\mr{GL}}: G_1^{\mr{t w}} \longrightarrow G^{\mr {phys }}~.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-046: \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-GL-Twist}  \varphi_1^{\mr{GL}}: \wt{G}_1^{\mr {tw}} \longrightarrow \wt{G}^{\mr {phys }} \big(\left(u_1, u_2\right), e^{i \theta},g\big) \longmapsto\big(\left(u_1, u_2\right), \mr{diag}\big(e^{i \theta} u_1, e^{-i \theta} u_2\big),g\big)~, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-379: The $\mr{U(1)_R}$ factor in $\wt{G ^{\mr{t w }$ reflects the fact that a $\mr{U(1)}$ subgroup of $\mr{SU(4)}$ commutes with the image of the first homomorphism $\mr{Spin(4) \longrightarrow \mr{S U(4)}$ and hence is a residual $\mr{R}$-symmetry of the twisted theory.}}}
elements.sent-2411-380: Define \begin{equation}\label{eq:G-and-C-DW-Twist} \begin{aligned} \wt{G}_2^{\mr{tw}}= \mr{Spin(4)} \times \mr{U(2)_R}\times \wt{G}^{\mr{gauge}} ~, C_2^{\mr{t w}}=\left\{(a, a, 1, g):(a, a, a, g) \in C^{\mr {phys }}\right\}~. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The twisting group is $G_2^{\mr{t w =\wt{G _2^{\mr{t w / C_2^{\mr{t w }$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}\label{eq:G-and-C-DW-Twist}  \wt{G}_2^{\mr{tw}}= \mr{Spin(4)} \times \mr{U(2)_R}\times \wt{G}^{\mr{gauge}} ~, C_2^{\mr{t w}}=\left\{(a, a, 1, g):(a, a, a, g) \in C^{\mr {phys }}\right\}~. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-381: The homomorphism is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-DW-Twist} \begin{aligned} \varphi_2^{\mr{DW}}: \wt{G}_2^{\mr {tw}} \longrightarrow \wt{G}^{\mr {phys }} \big(\big(u_1, u_2\big), u, g\big) \longmapsto\big(\big(u_1, u_2\big), \mr{diag}\big(\tfrac{u_2}{\sqrt{\mr{det} u}}, u\big), g\big)~. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Then $\varphi_2^{\mr{DW }$ descends to a homomorphism \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-DW-Twist-bar} \ov{\varphi}_2^{\mr{DW}}: G_2^{\mr{t w}} \longrightarrow G^{\mr {phys }}~.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-DW-Twist}  \varphi_2^{\mr{DW}}: \wt{G}_2^{\mr {tw}} \longrightarrow \wt{G}^{\mr {phys }} \big(\big(u_1, u_2\big), u, g\big) \longmapsto\big(\big(u_1, u_2\big), \mr{diag}\big(\tfrac{u_2}{\sqrt{\mr{det} u}}, u\big), g\big)~. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-382: The $\mr{U(2)_R}$ factor in $\wt{G ^{\mr {tw }$ reflects residual $\mr{R}$-symmetry of the twisted theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Define \begin{equation}\label{eq:G-and-C-VW-Twist} \begin{aligned} \wt{G}_3^{\mr {tw }}=\mr{Spin(4)} \times \mr{SU(2)}_R\times \wt{G}^{\mr{gauge}} ~, C_3^{\mr{tw}}=\left\{(\zeta, \zeta, 1, g):(\zeta, \zeta, \zeta, g) \in C^{\mr {phys }}\right\}~. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The twisting group is $G_3^{\mr{t w =\wt{G _3^{\mr{t w / C_3^{\mr{t w }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-384: To define the twisting homomorphism, introduce the map \begin{equation} f: \mr{SU(2)} \times \mr{SU(2)_R} \longrightarrow \mr{SU(4)_R} \left(u,\left(\begin{smallmatrix} \alpha \beta -\bar{\beta} \bar{\alpha} \end{smallmatrix}\right)\right) \longmapsto\left(\begin{smallmatrix} \alpha u \beta u \bar{\beta} u \bar{\alpha} u \end{smallmatrix}\right)~. \end{equation} It is easy to check that $f$ is a well-defined homomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-385: We define the twisting homomorphism by \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-VW-Twist} \begin{aligned} \varphi_3^{\mr {VW}}: \wt{G}_3^{\mr{tw}} \longrightarrow \wt{G}^{\mr {phys }} \left(\left(u_1, u_2\right), u, g\right) \longmapsto\left(\left(u_1, u_2\right), f\left(u_2, u\right), g\right)~. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Clearly, $\varphi_3^{\mr{VW }$ descends to a homomorphism \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-VW-Twist-bar} \ov{\varphi}_3^{\mr{VW}}: G_3^{\mr{t w}} \longrightarrow G^{\mr {phys }}~.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}\label{eq:varphi-VW-Twist}  \varphi_3^{\mr {VW}}: \wt{G}_3^{\mr{tw}} \longrightarrow \wt{G}^{\mr {phys }} \left(\left(u_1, u_2\right), u, g\right) \longmapsto\left(\left(u_1, u_2\right), f\left(u_2, u\right), g\right)~. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-386: Again, the twisted theory has residual $\mr{S U(2)_R~ R}$-symmetry.

elements.sent-2411-412: There is no off-shell formulation of an untwisted $\CN=2$ hypermultiplet with a finite number of auxiliary fields, but there exists one for the twisted hypermultiplet \cite{Karlhede:1988ax .\footnote{The fact that the untwisted off-shell $\CN=2$ hypermultiplet requires infinitely many auxiliary fields is perhaps most elegantly seen in the formalism of projective superspace \cite{Lindstrom:1989ne,Lindstrom:2009afn : here, the hypermultiplet superfield is an infinite series in a twistorial coordinate $\zeta \in \IC\IP^{1}$, with infinitely many coefficients corresponding to the infinitely many auxiliary fields.}}}

elements.sent-2411-437: The response currents to variations of the background metric and background $\mr{U(1)}$ connections are $\CQ$-exact as a result of $\CQ$-exactness of the action.
elements.sent-2411-438: Thus the twisted partition function and topological correlators are formally locally constant as a function of the background connections.
elements.sent-2411-439: Given that the action is $\CQ$-exact up to a topological term, one can show that the path integral localizes to supersymmetric backgrounds i.e., solutions of $\CQ$-fixed equations with fermions set to zero.
elements.sent-2411-440: From \eqref{eq:sQonfields , we see that supersymmetric configurations correspond to the solutions of the following $\CQ$-fixed equations: \begin{equation}\left. {l}D_{\dt{\alpha} \dt{\beta}}^{a} =F_{\dt{\alpha} \dt{\beta}}^{a} ~, h_{\alpha u} =0 ~, \wt{h}^u_\alpha=0 ~,  \right\}\quad {l} a=1,\ldots,\mr{dim}(G^{\mr{gauge}}) ~, u=1,\ldots,\mr{rank}(\IT^{\mr{f}}) ~. \end{equation} Setting the auxiliary fields of the dynamical vectormultiplets and hypermultiplets to their equations of motion \begin{equation}\left. {l}D_{\dt{\alpha}\dt{\beta}}^{a} =2 \wt{M}^u_{(\dt{\alpha}} T_\varrho^{a} M_{\dt{\beta})u} ~, h_{\alpha v} =\sigma_{\alpha \dt{\alpha}}^\mu D_\mu M^{\dt{\alpha}}_v ~, \wt{h}^v_\alpha = -D_\mu \wt{M}^{\dt{\alpha}v} \wt{\sigma}_{\dt{\alpha} \alpha}^\mu~,  \right\}\quad {l} $u$ summed over ~, v=1,\ldots,\mr{rank}(\IT^{\mr{f}}) ~, \end{equation} yields \begin{equation}\label{eq:nonabmoneq} \begin{aligned} \left. \begin{array}{l} F_{\dt{\alpha} \dt{\beta}}^{a}-2 \wt{M}^u_{(\dt{\alpha}} T_\varrho^{a} M_{\dt{\beta})u} =0 ~, \sigma_{\alpha \dt{\alpha}}^\mu D_\mu M^{\dt{\alpha}}_v =0 ~, \sigma_{\alpha \dt{\alpha}}^\mu D_\mu \wt{M}^{\dt{\alpha}v} = 0 ~, \end{array} \right\} \quad \begin{array}{l} \text{$u$ summed over} ~, v=1,\ldots,\mr{rank}(\IT^{\mr{f}}) \end{array}~. \end{aligned}\end{equation} These $\CQ$-fixed equations are called non-Abelian monopole equations \cite{Labastida:1995zj,Hyun:1995mb,Losev:1997tp,pidstrigach1995localisation,Witten:1999QFT2 and are generalizations of both the usual ASD instanton equations and the ``monopole'' or ``Seiberg-Witten'' equations \cite{Witten:1994cg .

elements.sent-2411-449: This allows us to generalize the non-Abelian monopole equations to any compact, simple gauge group.
elements.sent-2411-450: The path integral for topological correlators and the partition function formally localizes to the moduli space of non-Abelian monopole equations.
elements.sent-2411-451: After performing the path integral, we obtain topological invariants which depend on the first Chern classes $c_1(u)$ and the background fields $\rho_u(\mu(b))$ for 1-form symmetries constrained according to \eqref{eq:BackgroundGerbeCondition .}

elements.section-2411-008: Topological Twisting Of General Class $\CS$ Theories
elements.sent-2411-454: We now turn to class $\CS$ theories.
elements.sent-2411-455: In general, we cannot apply the general discussion of Sections \ref{sec:NonSpin -\ref{sec:backind because in general class $\CS$ theories are non-Lagrangian.}}
elements.sent-2411-456: As noted in \cite{Gaiotto:2009hg,Gaiotto:2009we weak coupling regimes are determined by a pants decomposition of the ultraviolet curve $C_{g,n}$ used to define the theory.}
elements.sent-2411-457: Here $g$ is the genus and $n$ the number of punctures.
elements.sent-2411-458: Recall that a pants decomposition of a stable Riemann surface $C_{g,n}$ \cite{HatcherExpos is a maximal collection of pairwise disjoint simple closed curves (also called cutting curves or cuffs).\footnote{A simple closed curve on $\IX$ is a map $c: [0,1] \to \IX$ that is closed, i.e., $c(0) = c(1)$, and also injective as a map $[0,1) \to \IX$.}}
elements.sent-2411-459: Following \cite{Manschot:2021qqe , we will refer to cutting curves surrounding the punctures as external cutting curves, and cutting curves $c_i$ associated with gauge groups as internal cutting curves.}
elements.sent-2411-460: In the mathematical literature, only the internal cutting curves are counted as cuffs.
elements.sent-2411-461: For a given connected surface $C_{g,n}$, any pants decomposition will consist of $(2g-2+n)$ pairs of pants.\footnote{The Euler characteristic $\chi(C_{g,n ) = 2-2g-n$ of a stable Riemann surface is negative, this excludes $C_{0,0}$, $C_{0,1}$, $C_{0,2}$, and $C_{1,0}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-462: Strictly speaking, one should distinguish between boundary components and punctures (the latter being marked points in the interior of the closure of the surface).
elements.sent-2411-463: Here the $n$ ``punctures'' are obtained by removing $n$ small open disks from the surface.
elements.sent-2411-464: Moreover, any pants decomposition consists of $(3g-3+n)$ internal cutting curves and $n$ external cutting curves.
elements.sent-2411-465: We denote a typical pants decomposition by a list of cutting curves: \begin{equation}\label{eq:pants-decom-list} \mf{p} = \{c_1,\ldots,c_{3g-3+2n}\}~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-466: The complement of these curves in $C_{g,n}$ is a collection of $(2g-2+n)$ pairs of pants\footnote{A pair of pants (hereafter abbreviated simply to ``pants'') is a surface homeomorphic to $C_{0,3}$.. (also called trinions).}
elements.sent-2411-467: In these regimes we can express the theory as a gauge theory, coupled to possibly non-Lagrangian theories associated with the trinions.
elements.sent-2411-468: The key point is that we need only study general properties of the partition functions of these (possibly non-Lagrangian) trinion theories in the presence of background fields.
elements.sent-2411-469: This is all that is needed to discuss the analogues of the cohomological conditions \eqref{eq:BackgroundGerbeCondition and the twisting procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-470: The trinion theory\footnote{As noted in Section \ref{sec:a1classS , the trinion theory is a class $\CS$ theory on a three-punctured sphere.}}
elements.sent-2411-471: The notation $T[\mathfrak{g , D_{p_1 , D_{p_2 , D_{p_3 ]$ specifies the gauge algebra of the 6d theory and the puncture data $D_{p_1 ,D_{p_2 , D_{p_3}$ at the three punctures.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-472: The data at a regular puncture $p$ includes a reductive Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g _p$ and a homomorphism $\rho: \mf{su(2) \to \mathfrak{g _p$, among other things.}}}
elements.sent-2411-473: The flavor symmetry algebra $\mathfrak{f _{p ^{\mr{fl }$ at puncture $p$ is given by the centralizer of the image of $\rho$ in $\mathfrak{g _{p}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-474: See \cite{Chacaltana:2012zy,Tachikawa:2013kta,Tachikawa:2015bga , for a more complete discussion of the punctures in class $\CS$ theories.}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Transformation Of Cohomological Conditions Under S-duality
elements.sent-2411-607: We next analyze the effect of S-duality transformations on these cohomological conditions.
elements.sent-2411-608: Recall that the S-duality group of the theory is the mapping class group $\mr{MCG (C_{g,n )$ and acts on the space of gauge couplings of the theory (identified with the Teichm\"uller space of $C_{g,n}$, see \cite{Witten:1997sc,Gaiotto:2009hg,Gaiotto:2009we ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-609: The set of equivalence classes of pants decompositions modulo the action of the mapping class group $\mr{MCG (C_{g,n )$ is finite.\footnote{Note that $\mr{MCG (C_{g,n )$ is isomorphic to the automorphism group of the pants graph or pants complex of $C_{g,n}$, see \cite{Margalit for a proof.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-610: This is the pants complex of Hatcher and Thurston \cite{HatcherThurston1980 with the inclusion of 2-cells by Hatcher, Lochak, and Schneps \cite{hatcher2000teichmuller .}}
elements.sent-2411-611: The resulting pants complex is connected and simply connected.
elements.sent-2411-612: Thus, there are finitely many S-duality orbits of theories based on $C_{g,n}$.
elements.sent-2411-613: We will show that there is a natural transformation of the cohomological conditions of theories in the same S-duality orbit.
elements.sent-2411-614: To do so, we introduce the graph of a pants decomposition of $C_{g,n}$.
elements.sent-2411-615: Given a pants decomposition $\mf{p}$, we define an undirected graph $G(\mf{p )$ of $\mf{p}$ with vertices as the pairs of pants and external cutting curves in $\mf{p}$, such that two pairs of pants are connected by an edge in $G(\mf{p )$ if they are bounded by a common internal cutting curve.}}
elements.sent-2411-616: Further, an external cutting curve (a vertex in $G(\mf{p )$) is connected by an edge in $G(\mf{p )$ to that pair of pants which it bounds (also a vertex in $G(\mf{p )$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-617: Thus $G(\mf{p )$ has $2g-2+n$ trivalent vertices, $n$ univalent vertices and $3g-3+2n$ edges.}
elements.sent-2411-618: An illustrative example is shown in Figure \ref{fig:ptoG(p) .}

elements.sent-2411-619: Two pants decompositions $\mf{p}$, $\mf{p '$ are in the same $\mr{MCG (C_{g,n )$-orbit if and only if the corresponding graphs $G(\mf{p )$, $G(\mf{p ')$ are isomorphic \cite{wolf .\footnote{Two graphs $G(\mf{p )$ and $G(\mf{p ')$ are said to be isomorphic if there exists a bijection between their vertex sets $f: V\big(G(\mf{p )) \to V\big(G(\mf{p ')\big)$ such that any two vertices $v_1, v_2 \in V\big(G(\mf{p )\big)$ of $G(\mf{p )$ are adjacent in $G(\mf{p )$ if and only if their images $f(v_1)$ and $f(v_2)$ are adjacent in $G(\mf{p ')$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-659: In this appendix, we recall the notion of transfer of structure group.
elements.sent-2411-660: For simplicity, we will work in the category of smooth manifolds (where the objects are smooth manifolds and morphisms are smooth maps).
elements.sent-2411-661: Let $G$ be a Lie group and $\IX$ a smooth manifold.
elements.sent-2411-662: Let $\CC(G, \IX)$ be the groupoid of principal $G$-bundles over $\IX$.
elements.sent-2411-663: Let $G_1, G_2$ be two Lie groups.
elements.sent-2411-664: Given a homomorphism $\phi: G_1 \longrightarrow G_2$, we have a functor $\phi_*: \CC\left(G_1, \IX\right) \longrightarrow \CC\left(G_2, \IX\right)$ that maps a principal $G_1$-bundle $P_1 \stackrel{\pi_1 {\longrightarrow \IX$ to $P_1 \times_\phi G_2 \stackrel{\pi'_1 {\longrightarrow \IX$ where \begin{equation}P_1 \times_\phi G_2 := \left(P_1 \times G_2\right) / \sim_\phi ~,\end{equation} with equivalence relation is defined by \begin{equation}(p_1 \cdot h, g) \sim_\phi\left(p_1, \phi(h) g\right), \quad(p_1, g) \in P_1 \times G_2, h \in G_1 ~.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-116: \begin{equation}(p_1 \cdot h, g) \sim_\phi\left(p_1, \phi(h) g\right), \quad(p_1, g) \in P_1 \times G_2, h \in G_1 ~.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-665: The projection $\pi'_1: P_1 \times_\phi G_2 \longrightarrow \IX$ is defined as $\pi'_1([(p_1, g)])=\pi_1(p_1)$ and the right $G_2$ action is given by \begin{equation}[(p_1, g_1)] \cdot g_2=\left[\left(p_1,g_1\cdot g_2\right)\right] ~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-667: The transfer of structure group of $P_1 \stackrel{\pi_1 {\longrightarrow \IX$ from $G_1$ to $G_2$ using the homomorphism $\phi: G_1 \longrightarrow G_2$ is, by definition, the image $\phi_*(P_1 \stackrel{\pi_1 {\longrightarrow \IX)$ under the above functor.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-668: One can show that in terms of patches and transition functions, this means that if the local trivialization of $P_1 \xrightarrow{\pi_1 \IX$ is given by $\{U_p,t_{pq}\}$, then the local trivialization of $\phi_*(P_1 \stackrel{\pi_1 {\longrightarrow \IX)$ is given by $\{U_p,\wt{t _{pq}\}$ with $\wt{t _{pq = \phi\circ t_{pq $.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-669: A reduction of structure group of $P_2 \stackrel{\pi_2 {\longrightarrow \IX$ from $G_2$ to $G_1$ using the homomorphism $\phi: G_1 \longrightarrow G_2$ is a principal $G_1$-bundle $P_1 \xrightarrow{\pi_1}\IX$ together with an isomorphism of $\phi_*(P_1 \xrightarrow{\pi_1}\IX) $ with $P_2 \xrightarrow{\pi_2 \IX$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-670: One can show that in terms of patches and transition functions, this means that there is an atlas and local trivialization of $P_2 \xrightarrow{\pi_2 \IX$ given by $\{U_p,\wt{t _{pq}\}$ such that there exists a $G_1$ bundle $P_1\to \IX$ with local trivializations $\{U_p,t_{pq}\}$ such that $\wt{t _{pq = \phi\circ t_{pq}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-671: It is worth noting that there is never an obstruction to transferring the structure group of a principal bundle, but there are, in general, nontrivial obstructions to the existence of a reduction of structure group.
elements.sent-2411-672: Here are some examples of reductions of structure groups: Fix a positive integer $n \in \IZ_{> 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-673: Given an $n$-manifold $\IX$ and a structure group $G$, a $G$-structure \cite{ChernG on $\IX$ is a principal $G$-subbundle of the frame bundle $F\IX$.}
elements.sent-2411-674: Cases where the $G$-structure corresponds to standard tensor fields are familiar.
elements.sent-2411-675: For example, an $\mr{O(n)}$ structure defines a Riemannian metric on $\IX$ and can be viewed as a principal $\mr{O(n)}$ subbundle of the $\mr{GL(n,\IR)}$ frame bundle, or equivalently, a reduction of structure group from $\mr{GL(n,\IR)}$ to $\mr{O(n)}$ via the natural inclusion.
elements.sent-2411-676: A further reduction to $\mr{SO(n)}$ structure defines an orientation\footnote{An orientation without a choice of Riemannian metric corresponds to a reduction from $\mr{GL(n,\IR)}$ to $\mr{GL^{+ (n,\IR)}$, the subgroup of matrices of positive determinant.}}
elements.sent-2411-677: A volume form corresponds to a reduction from $\mr{GL(n, \IR)}$ to $\mr{SL(n,\IR)}$, the subgroup of matrices of determinant $+1$.
elements.sent-2411-678: Indeed, some of these reductions might not exist -- obstructions are quantified by certain characteristic classes.
elements.sent-2411-679: The aforementioned examples are of tangential structures, i.e., structure group reductions for the frame bundle.
elements.sent-2411-680: However, the idea of the reduction of structure group applies more generally to principal bundles.
elements.sent-2411-681: The notion of transfer of structure group can be extended to include the data of a connection.
elements.sent-2411-682: Let $\CC^{\mr{conn (G, \IX)$ be the groupoid of principal $G$-bundles with connection.}}

elements.sent-2411-790: Superconformal Multiplets In this section, we will briefly review superconformal multiplets for the $d = 4$ $\CN=2$ superconformal algebra $\mf{su (2,2|2)$.\footnote{This is the real form appropriate to $d=4$ Lorentzian signature, which is implicit in standard treatments of radial quantization.}}
elements.sent-2411-791: The appropriate real form for $d=4$ Euclidean signature is $\mathfrak{su^{* (4|2)}$, which would lead to a noncompact summand in the Euclidean $\mr{R}$-symmetry group.}
elements.sent-2411-792: This distinction will be unimportant for us, however, see \cite{Cushing:2023rha for a discussion in the context of topological twisting.}
elements.sent-2411-793: The reader is referred to \cite{Cordova:2016emh,Rastelli:2014jja,Dolan:2002zh,Eberhardt:2020cxo for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-794: The superconformal algebra admits a bosonic subalgebra, \begin{equation}\mf{su}(2,2) \oplus \mf{su}(2)_{\mr{R}} \oplus \mf{u(1)}_{\mr{R}} \subset \mf{su}(2,2|2) ~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-795: Note that $\mf{su (2,2) \cong \mf{so (4,2)$, and there is an $\mf{su (2)_{\mr{R }\oplus\mf{u (1)_{\mr{R }$ symmetry, we will denote the charge under the $\mr{R}$-symmetry by $(R;r)$ where $R\in\IZ_{\geq 0}$ is the Dynkin label for the $\mf{su (2)_{\mr{R }$ and $r\in\IR$ is the charge under $\mf{u (1)_{\mr{R }$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-796: The scaling dimension will be denoted by $\Delta$ and the Dynkin labels for the representation under the (spin lift of) the 4d rotation group $\mr{Spin(4)}\cong\mr{SU(2)}\times\mr{SU(2)}$ will be denoted by $(j_1;j_2)\in \IZ_{\geq 0}\times\IZ_{\geq 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-797: An operator with these eigenvalues under the generators of the superconformal algebra will then be denoted by $[j_1;j_2]_{\Delta ^{(R;r)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-798: In particular, the supercharges transform in the following representations: \begin{equation}\CQ \in[1 ; 0]_{\frac{1}{2}}^{(1 ;-1)}, \quad \ov{\CQ} \in[0 ; 1]_{\frac{1}{2}}^{(1 ; 1)}~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-799: The basic generating multiplets are the long multiplets and short multiplets satisfying certain unitarity bounds \cite{Minwalla:1997ka .}
elements.sent-2411-800: Short multiplets are obtained from the long multiplet by imposing certain shortening conditions.
elements.sent-2411-801: These multiplets are listed in Tables \ref{tab:Q-short and \ref{tab:Q_bar-short .. \begin{small.}}}

elements.sent-2411-822: In this appendix, we review the superconformal index.
elements.sent-2411-824: First, let us review the supersymmetric index as a trace over the Hilbert space in the Hamiltonian quantization of a generic supersymmetric theory in $d$ spacetime dimensions, coupled to conserved currents of some global symmetries (i.e., to background flavor bundles).
elements.sent-2411-825: To compute it, we pick a supercharge\footnote{In terms of background supergravity, this is a choice of generalized Killing spinor, i.e., a solution to the differential constraints obtained by demanding the vanishing of the supersymmetry variations of the gravitino, in a bosonic background where the auxiliary fields of the supergravity multiplet may have nontrivial profiles \cite{Festuccia:2011ws,Klare:2012gn,Cassani:2012ri,Klare:2013dka,Karlhede:1988ax,Cushing:2023rha .}}
elements.sent-2411-826: In a unitary theory, this implies that the spectrum of $\mr{H}$ is positive semidefinite.
elements.sent-2411-827: The index is simply a weighted supertrace of states in $\Ker \,\mr{H}$, i.e., states in the cohomology of $\CQ$ and $\CQ^+$, or equivalently, the Hilbert space of a radially quantized theory on $\IS^{d-1}\times \IR$.
elements.sent-2411-828: Concretely, the index has a trace formula of the form \begin{equation}\CI(\{ \mu_i \}) = \mr{Tr}_{\Ker\,\mr{H}}(-1)^{\mr{F}}e^{-\sum_i \mu_i T_i} e^{-2\beta\{\CQ, \CQ^+\}} ~, \label{eq:supIndex}\end{equation} where $\{T_i\}$ is a complete set of generators that commute with $\CQ$ and with each other, $\{\mu_i\}$ is a set of ``fugacities,'' or ``chemical potentials'' for the associated global symmetries\footnote{At the moment, we are including superconformal fugacities (finitely many in number) as well as fugacities associated with flavor symmetries (which could be arbitrarily many in number, and which commute with all generators of the superconformal algebra).}
elements.sent-2411-829: We will shortly refine this definition.
elements.sent-2411-830: As is the case for the original Witten index \cite{Witten:1982df , only the states with $\mr{H = 0$ contribute to the index, and consequently the index does not depend on $\beta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-831: The index so defined is a function of the fugacities that couple to the conserved charges of the corresponding global symmetries.\footnote{Sometimes, the factor $e^{-\sum_{i}\mu_i T_i}$ is replaced by $\prod_{i}\wt{\mu _i^{C_i}$ with $\wt{\mu _i$ referred to as the fugacities and $C_i$ the conserved charges.}}}
elements.sent-2411-832: There are various refinements of the index, and specializations that are useful in different contexts, especially for probing enhanced supersymmetry \cite{Gadde:2011uv .}
elements.sent-2411-833: Expressed as a trace over the full Hilbert space $\CH$ of states on $\IS^3$ the $d=4$ $\CN=2$ superconformal index has the generic form \begin{equation}\label{eq:supcIndex} \CI(p, q, t; \{a_i\}) \nonumber := Tr_{\CH}(-1)^{\mr{F}} p^{\frac{1}{3}(\Delta + \frac{1}{2}j_1 - R - r) + \frac{1}{2}j_2} q^{\frac{1}{3}(\Delta + \frac{1}{2}j_1 - R - r) - \frac{1}{2}j_2} t^{\frac{1}{2}(R+r)} e^{-\beta(\Delta - j_1 - R + \frac{1}{2}r)}\prod_{i=1}^{\mr{rk\,}G^{\mr{f}}} a_{i}^{f_i} ~.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-850: While it is often remarked that the supersymmetric index is the partition function on $\IS^3 \times \IS^1$, a more precise statement is that it is proportional to the partition function up to a scheme-independent (and hence physical) multiplicative factor involving the supersymmetric Casimir energy \cite{Assel:2014paa,Lorenzen:2014pna,Assel:2015nca .}

elements.sent-2411-856: In practice, however, it is preferable to have a specialization that is computable with less effort and nevertheless contains useful information.
elements.sent-2411-857: One such specialization is the so-called Schur limit of the superconformal index, with the resulting index known as the Schur index.\footnote{Its name stems from the existence of closed-form expressions for the ``wavefunctions,'' in terms of Schur polynomials.}

elements.sent-2411-974: This gives the cohomological condition \begin{equation}\rho_s(w_2(\IX))-\iota(\mu(b_{c_{T,i}^{\mr{f}}}))+\iota(\mu(b_{c^{\mr{f}}_{T,\alpha}}))+\iota\circ r_{n_{c_{T,j}^{\mr{s}}}}(c_1(c_j^{\mr{s}})) =0\in H^2(\IX,\IZ_{[2,N]})~,\end{equation} where $r_{n}$ denotes reduction $\bmod~n$.
elements.sent-2411-975: If the trinion is bounded by two internal cutting curves $c_{T,\alpha ^{\mr{f , c_{T,\beta ^{\mr{f }$ and one external cutting curve $c_{T,i ^{\mr{s }$, the cohomological condition we get is \begin{equation}\rho_s(w_2(\IX))+\iota(\mu(b_{c_{T,\alpha}^{\mr{f}}}))-\iota(\mu(b_{c^{\mr{f}}_{T,\beta}}))+\iota\circ r_{n_{c_{T,i}^{\mr{s}}}}(c_1(c_{T,i}^{\mr{s}})) =0\in H^2(\IX,\IZ_{[2,N]})~.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-976: Note that in this case, we cannot have all three internal cutting curves.
